citation: Brunsgaard et al. (2011) - Evaluation of the Indoor Environment of Comfort Houses: Qualitative and Quantitative Approaches
description: Few-shot example for ecosystem service extraction.

relevant_snippets.text: thermal comfort and indoor air quality, daylight conditions and acoustic properties
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Lists all four ecosystem services evaluated in this study

relevant_snippets.text: The measurements show that there is a tendency towards high temperatures in all three cases
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Evidences thermal comfort (indoor temperature) as an ecosystem service measured in the case study

relevant_snippets.text: The charts in Table 4 show that there were no problems with the CO2 levels during the summer period, whereas the required CO2 levels were not met in cases 2 and 8 during the winter.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Evidences indoor air quality as an ecosystem service measured through CO2 and humidity levels

relevant_snippets.text: The graphs in Table 5 show that all cases fulfilled the requirement of a minimum 2% daylight factor, even at the back of the room.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Evidences daylight conditions as an ecosystem service measured through daylight factors

relevant_snippets.text: The measurements show that none of the cases satisfied the class B requirement of the Danish Building Regulations regarding the reverberation time without furniture
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Evidences acoustic properties as an ecosystem service measured through reverberation time and ventilation noise

ecosystem_services.name: thermal comfort (indoor temperature)
ecosystem_services.anchor: tendency towards high temperatures in all three cases

ecosystem_services.name: indoor air quality
ecosystem_services.anchor: no problems with the CO2 levels during the summer period

ecosystem_services.name: daylight conditions
ecosystem_services.anchor: all cases fulfilled the requirement of a minimum 2% daylight factor

ecosystem_services.name: acoustic properties
ecosystem_services.anchor: none of the cases satisfied the class B requirement

lesson: This paper evaluates four aspects of the indoor environment: thermal comfort (indoor temperature), indoor air quality (CO2 levels and relative humidity), daylight conditions, and acoustic properties (reverberation time and ventilation noise). These are the ecosystem services evidenced by the passive house design strategy. The model should map these to the taxonomy: thermal comfort → 'Regulation of temperature', air quality → 'Regulation of air quality', daylight → 'Solar energy' (provisioning), acoustics → 'Regulation of sound'. Do NOT extract the passive house design itself as an ecosystem service — that is the design strategy.
